title: A Therapist Explains Burnout
summary: A burnt-out client meets her therapist for a first session, and the conversation moves carefully from symptoms to the harder question of what actually has to change.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: I'm not sure where to start. I'm here because my doctor said I was burnt out, and I don't really know what that means. I just know I'm tired.

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: Starting with 'I'm tired' is fine. People come in saying that, and underneath it there's almost always more. Can you tell me what kind of tired? Tired after a long week is different from tired that doesn't go away.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: The kind that doesn't go away. I sleep eight hours and I still feel as if I haven't slept. I sit down at my desk on Monday morning and I'm already done.

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: That's a useful description. Sleep that doesn't restore is one of the clearest signals. Has there been a particular point in the last year when it started to feel different, rather than just busy?

body.speaker: Client
body.text: Probably last spring. We had a big launch, and I was the person holding it together. After it shipped, everyone else celebrated, and I just felt... empty. Then the next project started, and the same pattern began. I never came back up.

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: That's very common. Burnout is often described as the result of long stress, but more accurately it's the result of long stress without recovery. The launch worked because you spent a reserve. The reserve has not been refilled.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: That's exactly how it feels. So how do I refill it? I keep trying to take time off, and nothing changes.

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: Time off doesn't refill the reserve on its own. If you go on holiday but spend the whole week worrying about work, the time has cost you money without restoring anything. Recovery requires the absence of the demand, not the absence of the desk.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: That's quite a sharp distinction. I think I've been confusing the two for a while.

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: Most people do. There are usually three things to look at: workload, autonomy, and meaning. Workload is the easy one to see. Autonomy is whether you feel in control of how the work gets done. Meaning is whether the work still seems worth doing. Burnout is rare when all three are healthy. It's almost certain when all three are damaged.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: I think mine is autonomy. I can probably handle the workload — I've handled worse. But everything has to be approved by someone, and I feel I've stopped being trusted to make decisions I used to make easily.

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: That's an important insight. Loss of autonomy often shows up as exhaustion because the mental cost of constantly seeking approval is higher than the cost of the work itself.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: I hadn't thought of it that way. But what do I do with that? I can't just demand back the autonomy. My manager won't change overnight.

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: We're not going to fix that in this room. What we can do is identify which constraints are imposed and which ones you've added yourself. Some of what feels like external control is internal. You may be asking permission for things that no one would deny.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: That's a confronting thought. I think I do that. I send a draft email and ask if it's okay to send the actual one. I check three times whether a meeting can move.

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: If you can reduce that pattern even a little, you'll free up energy without changing your manager. It's not the whole solution, but it's a piece you control today.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: Is burnout actually treatable, or is it just something you manage forever?

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: Honestly, both. The acute version is treatable — sleep, real rest, reducing demand, and changing what can be changed. The underlying tendency, if you have one, tends to stay. People who burnt out once are more likely to burn out again, unless they learn the early signals. The good news is that the second time, the signals are usually clearer.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: What are the early signals?

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: For most people: poor sleep, irritability with small things, loss of interest in things you used to enjoy outside work, and a creeping sense that you're behind even when you're not. If two or three of those show up for more than a couple of weeks, that's the point to act, not later.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: I had all four six months ago. I should have come in then.

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: Most people don't come in then. They come in when something breaks. That's what we'll work on. For now, I want you to do one practical thing this week — make a list of decisions you ask permission for, and pick one to stop asking about. We'll talk about what happened next session.

body.speaker: Client
body.text: Okay. That feels manageable. Thank you for not telling me to take a holiday. Everyone else has.

body.speaker: Therapist
body.text: A holiday will help, eventually. But not until you've changed what brought you here. Otherwise you'll come back from it tired in exactly the same way.

annotations.meaningVi: kiệt sức (do làm việc quá độ)
annotations.exampleEn: My doctor said I was burnt out.
It's the kind of tired that doesn't go away.

annotations.meaningVi: giữ cho mọi thứ không sụp đổ
annotations.exampleEn: I was the person holding it together during the launch.

annotations.meaningVi: đã tiêu dùng hết khoản dự trữ (sức lực, năng lượng)
annotations.exampleEn: You spent a reserve. The reserve has not been refilled.

annotations.meaningVi: sự vắng mặt của áp lực, yêu cầu
annotations.exampleEn: Recovery requires the absence of the demand, not the absence of the desk.

annotations.meaningVi: khối lượng công việc, quyền tự chủ, và ý nghĩa (ba yếu tố)
annotations.exampleEn: Three things drive burnout: workload, autonomy, and meaning.
Loss of autonomy often shows up as exhaustion.
The mental cost of constantly seeking approval is high.

annotations.meaningVi: áp đặt từ ngoài và tự mình thêm vào
annotations.exampleEn: Identify which constraints are imposed and which you've added yourself.

annotations.meaningVi: ý nghĩ khiến phải đối diện thẳng (với bản thân)
annotations.exampleEn: That's a confronting thought, but I think it's true.
If you stop asking permission, you'll free up energy.

annotations.meaningVi: phiên bản cấp tính, đợt nặng
annotations.exampleEn: The acute version is treatable; the tendency stays.
Learn the early signals so you can act before it becomes acute.

annotations.meaningVi: dễ cáu kỉnh vì những chuyện nhỏ
annotations.exampleEn: Irritability with small things is a classic early signal.

annotations.meaningVi: cảm giác len lỏi, dần dần
annotations.exampleEn: A creeping sense that you're behind even when you're not.

annotations.meaningVi: có thể quản lý được, làm được

grammarNotes.title: Modal Verbs for Diagnosis and Advice
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại minh hoạ cách dùng các động từ khuyết thiếu trong tư vấn lâm sàng: 'Burnout is often described as...', 'Recovery requires the absence of the demand', 'You may be asking permission for things that no one would deny', 'You'll free up energy', 'I want you to do one practical thing'. Trong môi trường tư vấn, các cụm này (may, will, want you to) cho phép vừa khẳng định vừa giữ giọng điệu hợp tác.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue illustrates modals in clinical advice: 'Burnout is often described as...', 'Recovery requires the absence of the demand', 'You may be asking permission for things that no one would deny', 'I want you to do one practical thing'. In therapy, these forms (may, will, want you to) assert observations while keeping a collaborative tone.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect and Past Perfect for Chronic Conditions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người nói dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành và quá khứ hoàn thành để mô tả tình trạng kéo dài: 'I never came back up', 'The reserve has not been refilled', 'I had all four six months ago', 'People who burnt out once are more likely to burn out again'. Cấu trúc: have/has + V3 (hiện tại hoàn thành) và had + V3 (quá khứ hoàn thành). Dùng khi nói về điều bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn liên quan đến hiện tại.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Speakers use present perfect and past perfect for chronic conditions: 'I never came back up', 'The reserve has not been refilled', 'I had all four six months ago'. Form: have/has + past participle (present perfect) and had + past participle (past perfect). Use when describing something that began in the past and still affects the present.

translationVi:
Khách: Tôi không chắc bắt đầu từ đâu. Tôi đến vì bác sĩ bảo tôi bị kiệt sức, và tôi không thực sự biết điều đó nghĩa là gì. Tôi chỉ biết tôi mệt.

Chuyên gia: Bắt đầu bằng 'tôi mệt' là ổn. Người ta đến và nói vậy, và bên dưới gần như luôn có nhiều thứ hơn. Cô có thể kể loại mệt nào không? Mệt sau một tuần dài khác với mệt không hết.

Khách: Loại không hết. Tôi ngủ tám tiếng và vẫn cảm giác như chưa ngủ. Tôi ngồi vào bàn làm việc sáng thứ Hai và đã thấy hết hơi rồi.

Chuyên gia: Đó là mô tả hữu ích. Giấc ngủ không phục hồi là một trong những tín hiệu rõ nhất. Có thời điểm nào trong năm qua khi nó bắt đầu cảm giác khác đi, chứ không chỉ bận?

Khách: Có lẽ mùa xuân năm ngoái. Chúng tôi có một đợt ra mắt lớn, và tôi là người giữ cho mọi thứ không sụp đổ. Sau khi xong, mọi người ăn mừng, và tôi chỉ thấy... trống rỗng. Rồi dự án tiếp theo bắt đầu, và mô-thức cũ lặp lại. Tôi chưa bao giờ phục hồi.

Chuyên gia: Rất phổ biến. Kiệt sức thường được mô tả là kết quả của căng thẳng kéo dài, nhưng chính xác hơn là kết quả của căng thẳng kéo dài mà không có phục hồi. Đợt ra mắt thành công vì cô đã tiêu một khoản dự trữ. Khoản dự trữ đó chưa được nạp lại.

Khách: Đúng cảm giác là vậy. Vậy làm sao để nạp lại? Tôi cứ cố nghỉ ngơi mà không có gì thay đổi.

Chuyên gia: Nghỉ phép tự nó không nạp lại được khoản dự trữ. Nếu cô đi nghỉ nhưng cả tuần lo lắng về công việc, kỳ nghỉ đó đã tốn tiền của cô mà không phục hồi được gì. Sự phục hồi cần sự vắng mặt của áp lực, không phải sự vắng mặt của bàn làm việc.

Khách: Sự phân biệt khá sắc. Tôi nghĩ tôi đã nhầm hai thứ này một thời gian.

Chuyên gia: Hầu hết mọi người vậy. Thường có ba điều cần xem xét: khối lượng công việc, quyền tự chủ, và ý nghĩa. Khối lượng công việc dễ thấy. Tự chủ là liệu cô có cảm giác kiểm soát cách công việc được làm. Ý nghĩa là liệu công việc còn đáng làm. Kiệt sức hiếm khi xảy ra khi cả ba đều khoẻ. Gần như chắc chắn xảy ra khi cả ba đều bị tổn hại.

Khách: Tôi nghĩ vấn đề của tôi là tự chủ. Khối lượng công việc tôi có thể xử lý — tôi đã xử lý nặng hơn. Nhưng mọi thứ đều phải được ai đó duyệt, và tôi cảm thấy mình đã không còn được tin để quyết định những điều tôi từng quyết định dễ dàng.

Chuyên gia: Đó là một nhận thức quan trọng. Mất tự chủ thường biểu hiện như kiệt sức vì chi phí tinh thần của việc liên tục xin phép cao hơn chi phí của chính công việc.

Khách: Tôi chưa nghĩ theo cách đó. Nhưng tôi làm gì với điều đó? Tôi không thể đòi lại quyền tự chủ. Quản lý sẽ không thay đổi qua đêm.

Chuyên gia: Chúng ta sẽ không sửa điều đó trong căn phòng này. Điều ta có thể làm là xác định ràng buộc nào do bên ngoài áp đặt và ràng buộc nào cô tự thêm vào. Một phần của cái tưởng là kiểm soát bên ngoài thực ra là từ bên trong. Cô có thể đang xin phép cho những việc không ai từ chối.

Khách: Đó là ý nghĩ phải đối diện. Tôi nghĩ tôi làm vậy thật. Tôi gửi bản nháp email và hỏi có gửi email thực sự được không. Tôi kiểm tra ba lần xem cuộc họp có dời được.

Chuyên gia: Nếu cô giảm mô-thức đó dù chỉ một chút, cô sẽ giải phóng năng lượng mà không cần thay đổi quản lý. Đó không phải toàn bộ giải pháp, nhưng là một phần cô kiểm soát được hôm nay.

Khách: Kiệt sức có thực sự chữa được không, hay là điều phải quản lý mãi?

Chuyên gia: Thật ra, cả hai. Phiên bản cấp tính chữa được — ngủ, nghỉ thật, giảm áp lực, và thay đổi điều có thể thay đổi. Khuynh hướng nền, nếu cô có, có xu hướng ở lại. Người đã kiệt sức một lần dễ kiệt sức lần nữa, trừ khi học được các tín hiệu sớm. Tin tốt là lần thứ hai, tín hiệu thường rõ hơn.

Khách: Tín hiệu sớm là gì?

Chuyên gia: Với hầu hết mọi người: ngủ kém, dễ cáu vì chuyện nhỏ, mất hứng thú với những điều từng thích ngoài công việc, và cảm giác len lỏi rằng mình đang tụt hậu dù không phải vậy. Nếu hai ba điều xuất hiện hơn vài tuần, đó là lúc hành động, không phải muộn hơn.

Khách: Tôi có cả bốn cách đây sáu tháng. Lẽ ra tôi đến lúc đó.

Chuyên gia: Hầu hết mọi người không đến lúc đó. Họ đến khi một thứ gì đó vỡ. Đó là điều ta sẽ làm việc. Bây giờ, tôi muốn cô làm một điều thực tế tuần này — lập danh sách các quyết định cô đang xin phép, và chọn một việc để ngừng hỏi. Mình sẽ nói về điều xảy ra trong buổi tới.

Khách: Được. Cảm giác làm được. Cảm ơn vì không bảo tôi đi nghỉ phép. Mọi người khác đều đã bảo.

Chuyên gia: Một kỳ nghỉ sẽ giúp, cuối cùng. Nhưng không trước khi cô thay đổi điều đã đưa cô đến đây. Nếu không, cô sẽ quay về từ kỳ nghỉ mệt theo đúng cách cũ.

questions.prompt: Why did the client come to therapy?
options: Her doctor said she was burnt out
questions.explanation: The client says 'my doctor said I was burnt out.'
questions.hint: A specific external reason is given.

questions.prompt: What kind of tiredness does the client describe?
options: The kind that doesn't go away even after eight hours of sleep
questions.explanation: The client says 'I sleep eight hours and I still feel as if I haven't slept.'

questions.prompt: What event marks when the tiredness started?
options: A big launch last spring after which she felt empty
A move to a new city
questions.explanation: The client says 'Probably last spring. We had a big launch... After it shipped... I just felt... empty.'
questions.hint: A specific work event and emotional aftermath are described.

questions.prompt: How does the therapist redefine burnout?
options: The result of long stress without recovery
Working over forty hours a week
questions.explanation: The therapist says burnout is 'the result of long stress without recovery.'
questions.hint: Two elements — stress and absent recovery — are contrasted.

questions.prompt: Why doesn't time off automatically refill the reserve?
options: Because if you spend the holiday worrying about work, the demand is still present
Because the body needs work to recover
Because rest is impossible to schedule
questions.explanation: The therapist says 'If you go on holiday but spend the whole week worrying about work, the time has cost you money without restoring anything.'
questions.hint: The demand follows you even on holiday.

questions.prompt: What three factors does the therapist say drive burnout?
questions.explanation: The therapist says 'Workload is the easy one... Autonomy... Meaning.'

questions.prompt: Which factor does the client think is the main problem for her?
questions.explanation: The client says 'I think mine is autonomy... I've stopped being trusted to make decisions I used to make easily.'
questions.hint: The client identifies one of the three factors.

questions.prompt: Why does loss of autonomy show up as exhaustion?
options: Because tired employees lose autonomy first
Because the mental cost of constantly seeking approval is higher than the cost of the work itself
Because employees have to work longer hours without it
questions.explanation: The therapist says 'the mental cost of constantly seeking approval is higher than the cost of the work itself.'
questions.hint: The cost is mental, not physical.

questions.prompt: What does the therapist say about which constraints are imposed?
options: All constraints are imposed by managers
Some of what feels like external control is actually internal — you may be asking permission for things no one would deny
All constraints are imposed by the law
questions.explanation: The therapist says 'Some of what feels like external control is internal. You may be asking permission for things that no one would deny.'
questions.hint: The therapist identifies a self-imposed pattern.

questions.prompt: What four early signals does the therapist name?
options: Poor sleep, irritability with small things, loss of interest outside work, a creeping sense of being behind
questions.explanation: The therapist lists 'poor sleep, irritability with small things, loss of interest in things you used to enjoy outside work, and a creeping sense that you're behind.'
questions.hint: Four specific symptoms are listed in order.

questions.prompt: How many of those signals did the client have six months ago?
questions.explanation: The client says 'I had all four six months ago.'

questions.prompt: What practical task does the therapist set for the client this week?
options: Make a list of decisions she asks permission for and pick one to stop asking about
questions.explanation: The therapist says 'make a list of decisions you ask permission for, and pick one to stop asking about.'
questions.hint: It is a specific, small intervention.

questions.prompt: What overall view of burnout recovery does the therapist convey?
options: Burnout can be acutely treated, but the tendency may stay; lasting recovery requires changing the cause, not just resting
Burnout will fix itself in time
questions.explanation: The therapist says 'The acute version is treatable... The underlying tendency, if you have one, tends to stay' and that a holiday only helps 'after you've changed what brought you here.'
questions.hint: Combine her remarks on acute vs underlying with the closing point about holidays.

cloze.template:
Client: I'm here because my doctor said I was {{b1}} out. I just know I'm tired.
Therapist: Can you tell me what kind of tired? Tired after a long week is different from tired that doesn't go {{b2}}.
Client: The kind that doesn't go away. I sleep eight hours and I still feel as if I haven't slept.
Therapist: Sleep that doesn't restore is one of the clearest signals. Burnout is the result of long stress without {{b3}}. You spent a reserve, and the reserve has not been refilled.
Client: So how do I refill it? I keep trying to take time off.
Therapist: Time off doesn't refill the reserve on its own. Recovery requires the absence of the {{b4}}, not the absence of the desk.
Therapist: There are three things to look at: workload, {{b5}}, and meaning. Burnout is rare when all three are healthy.
Client: Mine is autonomy. Everything has to be approved by someone.
Therapist: Loss of autonomy often shows up as exhaustion because the mental cost of constantly {{b6}} approval is higher than the cost of the work itself. Some of what feels like external control is internal. You may be asking permission for things no one would {{b7}}.
Client: I do that. I send a draft email and ask if it's okay.
Therapist: If you reduce that pattern, you'll {{b8}} up energy.
Client: Is burnout treatable?
Therapist: The acute version is. The underlying tendency tends to stay. Learn the early {{b9}}: poor sleep, irritability with small things, loss of interest outside work, a creeping sense of being behind.
Client: I had all four six months ago. I should have come in then.
Therapist: Most people don't come in then. Make a list of decisions you ask permission for, and pick one to stop {{b10}} about.

blanks.explanation: 'Burnt out' is the exact medical term — the past participle of 'burn out'.
'Doesn't go away' is the fixed phrase for persistent symptoms.
'Recovery' is the exact word the therapist uses — the active process of restoring, not just resting.
'Demand' is the exact word — referring to mental pressure rather than physical location.
'Autonomy' is the exact technical term used in workplace psychology.
'Seeking approval' is the standard phrase used in clinical contexts.
'No one would deny' is the exact phrase — denial is the verb the therapist uses for refusal of permission.
'Free up' is the exact phrasal verb — to release energy that was being absorbed by something else.
'Early signals' is the exact phrase the therapist uses — slightly less clinical than 'symptoms'.
'Stop asking about' is the exact phrase — connecting the practical task to the earlier pattern.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The therapist says many constraints we feel as external are actually self-imposed. Where in your own life might you be asking permission for things no one would deny — and what stops you from simply acting?

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: I'm not sure where to start. I'm here because my doctor said I was burnt out, and I don't really know what that means. I just know I'm tired.

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: Starting with 'I'm tired' is fine. People come in saying that, and underneath it there's almost always more. Can you tell me what kind of tired? Tired after a long week is different from tired that doesn't go away.

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: The kind that doesn't go away. I sleep eight hours and I still feel as if I haven't slept. I sit down at my desk on Monday morning and I'm already done.

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: That's a useful description. Sleep that doesn't restore is one of the clearest signals. Has there been a particular point in the last year when it started to feel different, rather than just busy?

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: Probably last spring. We had a big launch, and I was the person holding it together. After it shipped, everyone else celebrated, and I just felt... empty. Then the next project started, and the same pattern began. I never came back up.

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: That's very common. Burnout is often described as the result of long stress, but more accurately it's the result of long stress without recovery. The launch worked because you spent a reserve. The reserve has not been refilled.

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: That's exactly how it feels. So how do I refill it? I keep trying to take time off, and nothing changes.

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: Time off doesn't refill the reserve on its own. If you go on holiday but spend the whole week worrying about work, the time has cost you money without restoring anything. Recovery requires the absence of the demand, not the absence of the desk.

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: That's quite a sharp distinction. I think I've been confusing the two for a while.

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: Most people do. There are usually three things to look at: workload, autonomy, and meaning. Workload is the easy one to see. Autonomy is whether you feel in control of how the work gets done. Meaning is whether the work still seems worth doing. Burnout is rare when all three are healthy. It's almost certain when all three are damaged.

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: I think mine is autonomy. I can probably handle the workload — I've handled worse. But everything has to be approved by someone, and I feel I've stopped being trusted to make decisions I used to make easily.

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: That's an important insight. Loss of autonomy often shows up as exhaustion because the mental cost of constantly seeking approval is higher than the cost of the work itself.

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: I hadn't thought of it that way. But what do I do with that? I can't just demand back the autonomy. My manager won't change overnight.

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: We're not going to fix that in this room. What we can do is identify which constraints are imposed and which ones you've added yourself. Some of what feels like external control is internal. You may be asking permission for things that no one would deny.

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: That's a confronting thought. I think I do that. I send a draft email and ask if it's okay to send the actual one. I check three times whether a meeting can move.

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: If you can reduce that pattern even a little, you'll free up energy without changing your manager. It's not the whole solution, but it's a piece you control today.

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: Is burnout actually treatable, or is it just something you manage forever?

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: Honestly, both. The acute version is treatable — sleep, real rest, reducing demand, and changing what can be changed. The underlying tendency, if you have one, tends to stay. People who burnt out once are more likely to burn out again, unless they learn the early signals. The good news is that the second time, the signals are usually clearer.

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: What are the early signals?

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: For most people: poor sleep, irritability with small things, loss of interest in things you used to enjoy outside work, and a creeping sense that you're behind even when you're not. If two or three of those show up for more than a couple of weeks, that's the point to act, not later.

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: I had all four six months ago. I should have come in then.

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: Most people don't come in then. They come in when something breaks. That's what we'll work on. For now, I want you to do one practical thing this week — make a list of decisions you ask permission for, and pick one to stop asking about. We'll talk about what happened next session.

sentences.speaker: Client
sentences.text: Okay. That feels manageable. Thank you for not telling me to take a holiday. Everyone else has.

sentences.speaker: Therapist
sentences.text: A holiday will help, eventually. But not until you've changed what brought you here. Otherwise you'll come back from it tired in exactly the same way.
